nodes.context: Irish republican paramilitary force that sought to end British rule in Northern Ireland and achieve a united Ireland.
The other faction resulting from the 1969 IRA split, opposed to the Provisional IRA's offensive campaign.
Splinter group formed from a split within the Provisional IRA, still active in the dissident Irish republican campaign.
Splinter group formed from a split within the Provisional IRA.
External source that aided the IRA's offensive campaign.
Basque separatist group listed as an ally in the infobox.

Irish American support group listed as an ally in the infobox.
Loyalist paramilitary group that opposed the IRA and targeted Catholics.
Loyalist paramilitary group listed as an opponent in the infobox.
Irish republican paramilitary force seeking to end British rule in Northern Ireland and achieve Irish reunification.
The other faction resulting from the 1969 split within the IRA; initially the majority faction.
Irish republican paramilitary group; members participated in the 1981 hunger strike.
Irish republican paramilitary force that sought to end British rule in Northern Ireland and create an independent socialist republic.
Marxist-oriented republican paramilitary group, the other faction in the 1969 split.
Marxist republican paramilitary group that emerged later.
Loyalist paramilitary group operating during the Troubles.
Loyalist paramilitary group operating during the Troubles.
Loyalist paramilitary group operating during the Troubles.
Irish republican paramilitary force seeking to end British rule in Northern Ireland and create an independent socialist republic.
The other faction resulting from the 1969 split within the IRA; initially the majority faction.
Basque separatist group that had links with the IRA, exchanging weapons and training.
Palestinian organization that supported the IRA in the 1970s with training and arms shipments.
Colombian guerrilla group that received training from the IRA in bomb-making techniques.
Irish republican paramilitary force active during the Troubles, sought to end British rule in Northern Ireland.
Cover name used by the IRA to claim responsibility for killings of drug dealers.
Dissident republican splinter group formed in 1997 by former Provisional IRA members.
Dissident republican splinter group formed in 1986 following a split in the republican movement.
Dissident republican splinter group formed in 2005/6 by defectors from the Provisional IRA.
Irish republican paramilitary group; some members later joined Óglaigh na hÉireann.
Vigilante group formed in Derry in 2008, included former Provisional IRA members.
Merger of Real IRA, Republican Action Against Drugs, and other groups in 2012.

edges.evidence_quote: The Provisional IRA emerged in December 1969, due to a split within the previous incarnation of the IRA and the broader Irish republican movement . It was initially the minority faction in the split compared to the Official IRA but became the dominant faction by 1972.
edges.context: The Provisional IRA split from the Official IRA in 1969, initially being the minority faction.

edges.evidence_quote: Several splinter groups have been formed as a result of splits within the IRA, including the Continuity IRA , which is still active in the dissident Irish republican campaign , and the Real IRA .
edges.context: The Continuity IRA is a splinter group that split from the Provisional IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: Several splinter groups have been formed as a result of splits within the IRA, including the Continuity IRA , which is still active in the dissident Irish republican campaign , and the Real IRA .
edges.context: The Real IRA is a splinter group that split from the Provisional IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: NORAID (Irish Americans) , Libya , PLO , ETA , FARC

edges.context: The UVF is listed as an opponent of the Provisional IRA in the infobox.

edges.context: The UDA is listed as an opponent of the Provisional IRA in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: a feud with the Official IRA in October and November 1975 that left eleven people dead.
edges.context: The Provisional IRA engaged in a violent feud with the Official IRA in 1975.

edges.evidence_quote: seven IRA and three Irish National Liberation Army members starved themselves to death in pursuit of political status.
edges.context: Members of both organizations participated together in the 1981 hunger strike.

edges.evidence_quote: The Provisional IRA emerged in December 1969, due to a split within the previous incarnation of the IRA and the broader Irish republican movement.
edges.context: The Provisional IRA split from the Official IRA in 1969.

edges.evidence_quote: This was in contrast to the Official IRA and the Irish National Liberation Army, both of which adopted clearly defined Marxist positions.
edges.context: The text contrasts the Provisional IRA's ideology with that of the Irish National Liberation Army.

edges.evidence_quote: The Provisional IRA emerged in December 1969, due to a split within the previous incarnation of the IRA and the broader Irish republican movement.
edges.context: The Provisional IRA split from the Official IRA in 1969.

edges.evidence_quote: The IRA had links with the Basque separatist group ETA. Maria McGuire states the IRA received fifty revolvers from ETA in exchange for explosives training.
edges.context: The IRA and ETA exchanged weapons and training.

edges.evidence_quote: The IRA received support from the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) in the 1970s, with volunteers attending training camps in the Middle East.
edges.context: The PLO provided training and arms to the IRA in the 1970s.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2001, three Irishmen, known as the Colombia Three, were arrested and accused of training Colombian guerrillas, the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC).
edges.context: The IRA trained FARC in bomb-making techniques.

edges.evidence_quote: The IRA targeted drug dealers with punishment shootings and ordered them to leave Ireland, and some were killed using the covername Direct Action Against Drugs.
edges.context: Direct Action Against Drugs was a cover name used by the IRA for killings.

edges.evidence_quote: The Real IRA was formed in November 1997 when senior Provisional IRA members, including quartermaster-general Michael McKevitt, resigned over acceptance of the Mitchell Principles.
edges.context: The Real IRA was formed by defectors from the Provisional IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: The oldest dissident group is the Continuity IRA, which formed in 1986 following a split in the republican movement, over the decision to allow members, if elected, to take seats in Dáil Éireann.
edges.context: The Continuity IRA formed from a split in the republican movement involving the Provisional IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2005/6 some Provisional IRA members defected and formed Óglaigh na hÉireann, which became active in 2009.
edges.context: Óglaigh na hÉireann was formed by defectors from the Provisional IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: This group also included former members of the Irish National Liberation Army and a faction that splintered from the Real IRA.
edges.context: Óglaigh na hÉireann included a faction that splintered from the Real IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: This group also included former members of the Irish National Liberation Army and a faction that splintered from the Real IRA.
edges.context: Óglaigh na hÉireann included former members of the Irish National Liberation Army.

edges.evidence_quote: RAAD, 'the IRA', and some smaller groups merged with the Real IRA in 2012 to form the New IRA.
edges.context: RAAD merged with the Real IRA to form the New IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: RAAD, 'the IRA', and some smaller groups merged with the Real IRA in 2012 to form the New IRA.
edges.context: RAAD merged with the Real IRA to form the New IRA.

edges.evidence_quote: RAAD, 'the IRA', and some smaller groups merged with the Real IRA in 2012 to form the New IRA.
edges.context: The Real IRA merged with RAAD and other groups to form the New IRA.
